article.title: Amazon Agrees to Buy Globalstar for $11.6 Billion, Adding Satellite Spectrum and Apple Partnership to Its LEO Ambitions
article.summary: Amazon will acquire satellite operator Globalstar at $90 per share, gaining Band 53 spectrum, 24 operational satellites, and an existing Apple iPhone connectivity deal as it races to challenge Starlink.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Amazon announced on April 14 that it will acquire satellite operator Globalstar for approximately $11.6 billion in a deal designed to accelerate its entry into the direct-to-device satellite connectivity market. The acquisition gives Amazon immediate access to licensed midband spectrum, an operational satellite constellation, and an existing commercial relationship with Apple, as the company works to close the gap with SpaceX's Starlink.

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/23-leo-broadband-race-heats-up-as-amazon-seeks-deadline-extension-and-rivals-scale-military-and-enterprise-offerings) on Amazon Leo's FCC deadline challenges and the broader LEO broadband race.

## Deal Structure

Under the agreement, Globalstar shareholders will receive $90 per share, representing a 23 percent premium over Globalstar's last unaffected closing price, according to [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/14/amazon-acquire-internet-satellite-operator-globalstar-11-6b/). Shareholders can elect to receive cash or Amazon stock at a ratio of 0.3210 shares per Globalstar share, capped at $90 per share, with cash elections limited to 40 percent of total shares outstanding, as detailed in [Amazon's official announcement](https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/company-news/amazon-globalstar-apple).

The deal includes a potential $110 million downward adjustment if Globalstar fails to meet certain operational milestones before closing, which is expected in 2027 pending regulatory approval, according to [Amazon's announcement](https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/company-news/amazon-globalstar-apple). Globalstar will operate as an Amazon subsidiary after the transaction closes.

## What Amazon Gets

Globalstar's most strategically valuable asset is its Band 53 spectrum, covering 2483.5 to 2495 MHz, which [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/14/amazon_globalstar_satellite_envy/) described as "exclusive access" to midband spectrum "optimized for high-performance, low-latency, interference-free connectivity." This spectrum enables direct-to-device satellite communication without requiring a dish antenna.

The acquisition also brings 24 operational satellites in low Earth orbit plus 48 newly commissioned satellites equipped with beamforming technology, according to [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/14/amazon-acquire-internet-satellite-operator-globalstar-11-6b/). Globalstar invested approximately $800 million in the new satellites.

Perhaps most immediately consequential is the Apple relationship. Globalstar currently powers Emergency SOS via satellite on iPhone 14 and later models as well as Apple Watch, providing emergency text messaging, roadside assistance, and location sharing in areas without cellular coverage. Apple holds a 20 percent stake in Globalstar and previously provided a $1.5 billion cash infusion that accounts for the majority of Globalstar's revenue, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/14/amazon_globalstar_satellite_envy/).

As part of the acquisition, Amazon and Apple signed an agreement for Amazon Leo to continue powering satellite connectivity on iPhone and Apple Watch, with plans for joint development of new services, according to [Amazon's announcement](https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/company-news/amazon-globalstar-apple).

## Amazon Leo's Broader Strategy

Amazon Leo, formerly known as Project Kuiper, has 241 satellites in orbit as of the announcement, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/14/amazon_globalstar_satellite_envy/). The company's initial broadband constellation targets more than 3,200 satellites with commercial service planned for later in 2026, serving customers including Delta Airlines, AT&T, Vodafone, Australia's NBN, and NASA, as reported by [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/14/amazon-to-buy-globalstar-for-11-57b-in-bid-to-flesh-out-its-satellite-biz/).

The Globalstar acquisition feeds into a separate direct-to-device system that Amazon plans to deploy beginning in 2028. This system will deliver voice, data, and messaging services directly to standard mobile phones using thousands of additional satellites, with capacity to support hundreds of millions of customer endpoints, according to [Amazon's announcement](https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/company-news/amazon-globalstar-apple).

Amazon SVP Panos Panay said the combination will deliver "faster, more reliable service in more places" by uniting Globalstar's expertise with Amazon's customer focus, as reported by [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/14/amazon-acquire-internet-satellite-operator-globalstar-11-6b/).

## What We Don't Know

Several questions remain unanswered. Amazon has not disclosed consumer pricing for its satellite broadband service. The FCC has not yet ruled on Amazon's request for a two-year extension on its satellite deployment deadline, originally set for July 2026 with fewer than half the required satellites in orbit. It is also unclear how Amazon's 241-satellite constellation will compete on coverage and capacity against Starlink's fleet of more than 10,000 operational satellites across roughly 150 countries, as noted by [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/14/amazon-to-buy-globalstar-for-11-57b-in-bid-to-flesh-out-its-satellite-biz/) and [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/14/amazon_globalstar_satellite_envy/).

The regulatory approval process for the acquisition itself could introduce additional delays. Whether Amazon can integrate Globalstar's CDMA-based infrastructure with its own constellation architecture within the planned timeline remains to be seen.
